ynjtK79qJF.title: Experiment Setup
slides:
# GC Fairness: 142 SM vs 16 SM

*Shubhaankar*

# Experiment Setup

Used **CUDA MPS** on RTX 6000 Ada to limit process to 16 SMs.

| | Full GPU | MPS-Constrained |
|---|---|---|
| Available SMs | 142 | 16 (MPS 12%) |
| pGC: plax | 22 groups = 44 SMs | 2 groups = 4 SMs |
| pGC: aortic | 22 groups = 44 SMs | 2 groups = 4 SMs |
| pGC: bmode | 26 groups = 52 SMs | 3 groups = 6 SMs |
| pGC: default ctx | 1 group = 2 SMs | 1 group = 2 SMs |
| nGC | all 142 shared | all 16 shared |
| Configs | V1 Event nGC, V1 Event pGC | same |
| Frames | ~1600-20000 | ~1900 |

MPS daemon runs **inside** the container (host driver too old).

# Metrics: What We Measure **Spread** — per-frame unfairness across the 3 inference models. For each frame, take `max(GPU time) − min(GPU time)` across plax, aortic, bmode. | Spread | Meaning | |--------|---------| | 1 ms | All 3 models finished close together — fair | | 3 ms | One model finished 3ms after the fastest — moderate gap | | 5+ ms | One model was starved while another dominated — unfair | Since Holoscan has single-slot queues, the pipeline can only advance as fast as the **slowest model** in each frame. High spread = wasted time on the fast-finishing models.

# Metrics: Correlation Between Models **Pearson r** — measures linear co-movement between two models' GPU times across all frames. | r value | What it tells you | |---------|-------------------| | r ≈ 0 | Models are independent — one being slow doesn't affect the other | | r ≈ +0.9 | Both slow together — likely responding to same shared constraint | | r ≈ −0.5 | When one is fast, the other is slow — SM stealing | Pearson assumes a linear relationship and can be fooled by bimodal distributions (which we see under pGC at 16 SMs). **Spearman ρ** — same idea but ranks observations first. Asks "do the *orderings* agree?" instead of "do the *values* move linearly?" Robust to non-normal shapes. When Pearson and Spearman disagree, Spearman is more trustworthy.

# Metrics: Tail Ratio **P99/P50 ratio** — how much worse is the worst 1% of frames compared to the typical frame? | Ratio | Interpretation | |-------|---------------| | 1.0x | Worst case = typical case. Perfectly predictable. | | 1.1x | Worst 1% are 10% slower. Very stable. | | 1.3x | Worst 1% are 30% slower. Noticeable tail. | | 1.5x+ | Worst 1% are 50%+ slower. Significant outliers. | This is better than CoV for our data because CoV penalizes *any* variability, but for real-time pipelines what matters is bounding the worst case. A bimodal distribution can have high CoV but low P99/P50 if the worst case is bounded.

# Fairness Scatter: PLAX vs B-Mode ![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/1_fairness_scatter.png) Each dot is one frame. Pearson r annotated per panel. **142 SM (top row):** Both nGC and pGC show low correlation (~0). With 142 SMs there isn't meaningful contention — models are essentially independent regardless of policy. **16 SM (bottom row):** r jumps to ~0.9 in both nGC and pGC. PLAX and B-Mode are similar-sized models and both respond the same way to the shared 16-SM constraint. pGC does not break this coupling. However, looking at **Aortic** (different-sized model), Spearman ρ between Aortic-BMode is −0.35 under nGC vs −0.27 under pGC. The negative sign indicates SM competition — when Aortic is fast, BMode is slow. pGC partially reduces this.

# Per-Frame Spread ![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/2_spread_timeseries.png) Each point is one frame's spread (max − min GPU time across 3 models). Rolling average overlaid. | | nGC P50 | pGC P50 | nGC P99 | pGC P99 | |---|---------|---------|---------|---------| | 142 SM | 1.84 ms | 1.38 ms | 3.63 ms | 3.91 ms | | 16 SM | 2.01 ms | 1.75 ms | 3.61 ms | 3.32 ms | pGC reduces median spread at both GPU sizes — models finish more evenly. At 16 SMs, pGC also reduces the P99 spread, meaning the worst-case unfairness is bounded. Note: 142 SM event nGC has ~1600 frames vs pGC ~20000 — different x-axis scales. Compare the numbers, not the visual width.

# GPU Distributions: nGC vs pGC Overlaid ![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/3_overlaid_distributions.png) Blue fill = nGC. Orange line = pGC. Same axes per row. Dashed lines = P99. **142 SM (top):** pGC distributions are shifted right — models are slower because they only get 44-52 SMs instead of 142. This is the overhead cost of partitioning on a large GPU. **16 SM (bottom):** nGC and pGC distributions are nearly identical in position — both constrained to 16 SMs anyway. But the pGC P99 dashed lines sit left of the nGC P99 lines. pGC trims the right tail without shifting the median. At 16 SMs, isolation has zero median cost but still reduces worst-case latency.

# Frame Progress Heatmap

![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/4_frame_heatmap.png)

X = frame index (200 consecutive frames). Y = model. Color = GPU time. Color scale shared within each row so nGC and pGC panels are directly comparable.

Vertical dark lines hitting all 3 rows = shared slowdown (all models affected). Isolated dark cells in one row = unfairness (that model got starved).

At 16 SMs, both nGC and pGC show similar overall patterns — the high PLAX-BMode correlation from the scatter plot is visible here as coordinated color changes. Aortic (top row) is the most stable.

# Tail Ratio: P99/P50 ![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/5_tail_bounds.png) Lower = tighter tail = more predictable. | Model | 142 SM nGC | 142 SM pGC | 16 SM nGC | 16 SM pGC | |-------|-----------|-----------|----------|----------| | Aortic | 1.20x | 1.29x | 1.47x | 1.34x | | PLAX | 1.61x | 1.79x | 1.34x | 1.20x | | B-Mode | 1.17x | 1.19x | 1.34x | 1.14x | At 142 SMs, pGC makes tails worse for Aortic and PLAX — the overhead of fewer SMs adds scheduling noise. At 16 SMs, pGC improves tails for all 3 models (−9%, −10%, −15%). When SMs are scarce, isolation bounds worst-case contention without adding overhead.

# Cost vs Benefit by GPU Size ![](https://pub-6528bbdebaed4b9997412b6948ed7524.r2.dev/presentations/ynjtK79qJF/6_crossover_summary.png) **Top panel (overhead):** At 142 SMs, pGC costs +20-117% more GPU time per model. At 16 SMs, overhead drops to ~0%. **Middle panel (P99 improvement):** At 142 SMs, pGC makes P99 worse (lines below zero). At 16 SMs, pGC improves P99 by 8-17% (lines above zero). **Bottom panel (spread reduction):** pGC reduces per-frame spread at both sizes — 25% at 142 SMs, 13% at 16 SMs.

# Full Statistics | Metric | 142 SM nGC | 142 SM pGC | 16 SM nGC | 16 SM pGC | |--------|-----------|-----------|----------|----------| | GPU overhead | — | +20-117% | — | ~0% | | Spread P50 | 1.84ms | 1.38ms | 2.01ms | 1.75ms | | Spread P99 | 3.63ms | 3.91ms | 3.61ms | 3.32ms | | PLAX P99/P50 | 1.61x | 1.79x | 1.34x | 1.20x | | BMode P99/P50 | 1.17x | 1.19x | 1.34x | 1.14x | | Aortic P99/P50 | 1.20x | 1.29x | 1.47x | 1.34x | | PLAX-BMode Spearman ρ | 0.24 | 0.73 | 0.85 | 0.86 | | Aort-BMode Spearman ρ | 0.38 | 0.72 | −0.35 | −0.27 |

# Findings

1. **pGC has zero overhead at 16 SMs** — MPS already constrains the SM budget, so partitioning within it costs nothing.

2. **pGC reduces per-frame spread** — 13% at 16 SMs, 25% at 142 SMs. Models finish more evenly under both policies, but nGC is worse.

3. **pGC tightens tail latency at 16 SMs** — P99/P50 ratio improves 9-15% for all models. nGC has longer tails from uncontrolled contention.

4. **SM competition is visible between differently-sized models** — Aortic-BMode Spearman ρ is negative under nGC at 16 SMs (−0.35), confirming one model gains at the other's expense. pGC reduces this (−0.27).

5. **Similar-sized models stay correlated** — PLAX-BMode ρ ≈ 0.85 regardless of policy. Both respond to the same 16-SM constraint. GC isolates SMs but can't isolate shared memory bandwidth or L2 cache.

# Caveats - **MPS ≠ native small GPU.** Memory hierarchy, L2 cache, and bandwidth are still 142-SM class. Only SM count is restricted. Results on a real 16-SM GPU (Orin) may differ. - **1900 frames** at 16 SMs. P99 estimated from ~19 frames. Statistically thin. Need 20K+ frames for confidence. - **Single run per config**, no randomization, no confidence intervals. - **BGL limits Event E2E.** GPU-level improvements from pGC don't fully propagate to end-to-end latency because the CUDA driver mutex serializes all 8 Event threads.

# Next Steps

1. <mark>Flash Orin to JetPack 6</mark> — native 16 SM experiment without MPS artifacts

2. Sweep SM budgets (16, 24, 32, 48, 72 SMs) to map the overhead-benefit curve

3. Longer runs (20K frames) with multiple replicates + bootstrap CIs

4. Greedy scheduler comparison at 16 SMs (currently only Event has contention)
